body_txt:
Berets have long been used in Real Life owing to their practicality . Durability in all manner of weather, ease of manufacture and the ability to be dyed in various colors led to its use by landscape painters and later by other artists, causing it to be popularized as standard attire for the more creative types . These same characteristics have led to berets being adopted by armed and police forces for their uniforms. The use of berets in this context was made popular in the Second World War, though it goes back to as early as the 17th century, by Scottish soldiers, the Volontaires Cantabres, a French force raised in the Basque country in the 1740s to the 1760s, who also wore a blue beret, and the Carlist rebels, with their red berets, in 1830s Spain, although these were rather different from modern-day examples, having a much broader fabric flop.
The first military unit to officially use berets as part of the uniform were the French Chasseurs alpins , a unit dedicated to mountain and Winter Warfare in 1888, followed by the British Royal Tank Regiment adopting one in black in 1924, as it was found to be practical for use inside the tank, as it was compact, protected the head and hid oil stains. The wearing of berets of distinctive colors by elite special forces originated with the British Parachute Regiment , whose maroon beret was officially approved in July 1942, followed by the Commando Forces whose green beret was approved in October of that year. The United States Army Special Forces adopted a darker green beret in 1955, although it was not officially approved until 1961. Despite what fiction might tell you, this trope is more common than one would think, as several militaries ended up adopting berets as part of the rank-and-file uniform, with two ways to be worn; the standard way, pushed to the right (the flat part where the insignia goes oriented to the left and the fabric flop to the right), to free the shoulder that bears the rifle, or the French Pull, which is pulled to the other side. Since berets tend to have a unit insignia placed on their flat side, they can be used to show off some Bling of War without going overboard, while their format gives some Fashionable Asymmetry , helping a character stand out. Owing to the ties to Elites Are More Glamorous , expect those who wear to be highly competent soldiers, capable of walking through hell and back without flinching. After all, elites are elites for a reason. Expect its users to be referred by their beret color . They can also be used for leadership roles, especially for a Commanding Coolness or a Majorly Awesome , to help him stand out. Unlike the Commissar Cap , which its users can vary all the way between a General Failure and a Four-Star Badass , and again, due to links to elite units , leaders with berets tend, even at their worst, to be portrayed as decently competent leaders of men and very rarely incompetent buffoons , although it is not impossible for them to appear. Old Soldier -type characters can also use them, usually when retired at official ceremonies or events, as a good way to mark their veterancy. The more common colors for berets in fiction tend to be: Some more unorthodox colors exist, such as the raspberry colored berets for Airborne troops in the Soviet Union until 1969. Some militaries even use berets with a camo pattern, usually given to units specialized in Jungle Warfare . For other headgear with martial connotations, see Badasses Wear Bandanas and Fedora of Asskicking . For its more peaceful, creative cousin, see the Artsy Beret . Visually, the Artsy Beret is in general larger and floppier than the Martial Beret, and doesn't have a badge.
